title: Royal Australasian College of Surgeons (RACS)
metaTitle: RACS SIMG Assessment: Surgeon Specialist Pathway
metaDescription: How RACS assesses overseas-trained surgeons (SIMGs) for comparability across nine surgical specialties, the area-of-need pathway, and the road to FRACS.
summaryMarkdown: The Royal Australasian College of Surgeons (RACS) trains, examines and accredits surgeons across Australia and Aotearoa New Zealand, awarding Fellowship (FRACS) across nine surgical specialties. [[cite:racs-nine-specialties]] If you're an overseas-trained surgeon, RACS is the assessing authority the Medical Board relies on to decide whether your training and experience are comparable to an Australian and Aotearoa New Zealand trained surgeon. [[cite:racs-assessing-authority]] RACS runs an individualised SIMG assessment, placing you in one of three comparability outcomes and setting a period of supervised clinical assessment before recommending specialist recognition. [[cite:racs-three-outcomes]] Surgery is **not** currently on the Medical Board's [Expedited Specialist pathway](/pathways/expedited-specialist), so overseas-trained surgeons use the standard [specialist pathway](/pathways/specialist). [[cite:expedited-excludes-surgery]]

sections.heading: What RACS is and what FRACS means
sections.bodyMarkdown: RACS is responsible for surgical training, examination and professional development in Australia and Aotearoa New Zealand. Completing its training (or being assessed as comparable) leads to **Fellowship (FRACS)**, awarded across nine specialties: cardiothoracic, general, neurosurgery, orthopaedic, otolaryngology head and neck, paediatric, plastic and reconstructive, urology, and vascular surgery. [[cite:racs-nine-specialties]] For an overseas-trained surgeon, the Medical Board uses the college's comparability assessment when deciding whether to grant specialist registration, the college assesses and recommends; the Board registers. [[cite:mba-uses-college-recognition]] [[cite:racs-mba-decides-registration]]

sections.heading: The specialist assessment
sections.bodyMarkdown:
SIMGs seeking permanent specialist practice apply to RACS for a **specialist assessment** in their nominated specialty. [[cite:racs-specialist-pathway-purpose]] The process is:

1. **Document-based assessment**, RACS reviews the quality, quantity and scope of your clinical experience, qualifications, recency of practice and professional attributes against ANZ standards. [[cite:racs-document-assessment]]
2. **Assessment interview**, those who pass attend a structured online panel interview (commonly a specialty representative, a SET representative and a community member), exploring training, clinical decision-making, technical expertise, English communication and ethics. [[cite:racs-interview]]
3. **Comparability recommendation**, one of three outcomes, which determines next steps. [[cite:racs-three-outcomes]]

Once activated, the specialist assessment normally takes up to around three months. [[cite:racs-assessment-timeline]]

sections.heading: The three comparability outcomes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- **Substantially comparable**, awarded with evidence of recent surgical practice and a training program comparable to RACS, plus an exit exam comparable to the RACS Fellowship Examination (or sufficient standard/duration of practice to waive it). These SIMGs do **up to 12 months** of clinical assessment, starting under Medical Board Level 3 supervision and progressing to Level 4, with professional development and any up-skilling; successful completion entitles them to specialist recognition and Fellowship. [[cite:racs-substantially-comparable]]
- **Partially comparable**, recent practice demonstrated but a comparable exit exam or sufficient depth/duration of practice is missing. These SIMGs do **up to 24 months** of supervised practice under Level 3 supervision, complete up-skilling/courses, and must pass the RACS **Fellowship Examination**. [[cite:racs-partially-comparable]]
- **Not comparable**, no pathway to Fellowship on this assessment; applicants are advised to undertake further training, liaise with AHPRA, and may apply for the RACS SET program if eligible. [[cite:racs-not-comparable]]

The supervised clinical assessment period is how RACS confirms competency and addresses gaps before recommending specialist recognition. [[cite:racs-clinical-assessment-purpose]]

sections.heading: Area of need, and the standard specialist pathway
sections.bodyMarkdown:
RACS also offers an **area of need (AoN)** assessment, tied to a specific specialist-level hospital position a state or territory has classified as an area of need, evaluating both the applicant's suitability and whether the position has adequate resources. [[cite:racs-aon-position-specific]] An AoN assessment normally takes up to around four months. [[cite:racs-aon-timeline]]

RACS assessment sits inside the Medical Board's standard [specialist pathway](/pathways/specialist): the college assesses comparability and the Board decides registration. [[cite:mba-uses-college-recognition]] Surgery is not currently on the Board's [Expedited Specialist pathway](/pathways/expedited-specialist), as of mid-2026 that list covered specialties such as general practice, anaesthesia, psychiatry and obstetrics and gynaecology, with general surgery and otolaryngology only flagged as priorities being considered. [[cite:expedited-excludes-surgery]]

claims.text: RACS awards Fellowship (FRACS) across nine surgical specialties: cardiothoracic, general, neurosurgery, orthopaedic, otolaryngology head and neck, paediatric, plastic and reconstructive, urology, and vascular surgery.

RACS is the assessing authority that evaluates whether an overseas-trained surgeon's training and experience are comparable to an Australian and Aotearoa New Zealand trained surgeon for specialist recognition.
RACS recommends one of three comparability outcomes: not comparable, partially comparable, or substantially comparable.

Surgery is not currently on the Medical Board's Expedited Specialist pathway accepted list; as of 2026 it covered specialties such as general practice, anaesthesia, psychiatry and obstetrics and gynaecology, with general surgery and otolaryngology only flagged as priorities under consideration.
The Medical Board relies on the relevant specialist college's comparability assessment when deciding whether to grant specialist registration.
RACS advises on suitability and the requirements for specialist recognition, but the Medical Board makes the registration decision.
The RACS specialist pathway is for overseas-trained specialists seeking to work permanently as a specialist surgeon, who apply for a specialist assessment or area of need assessment.
RACS conducts a document-based assessment of the quality, quantity and scope of clinical experience, qualifications, recency of practice and professional attributes against ANZ standards.

SIMGs who pass the document stage attend a structured online panel interview (commonly a specialty representative, a SET representative and a community member) assessing training, decision-making, technical expertise, English and ethics.
Once activated, the RACS specialist assessment normally takes up to around three months.

Substantially comparable SIMGs do up to 12 months of clinical assessment (Medical Board Level 3 to 4) with professional development and any up-skilling, and on completion are entitled to specialist recognition and Fellowship.
Partially comparable SIMGs do up to 24 months of supervised practice under Level 3 supervision, complete up-skilling and courses, and must pass the RACS Fellowship Examination.
Not comparable SIMGs are not offered a pathway to Fellowship on that assessment; they are advised to undertake further training, liaise with AHPRA, and may apply for the RACS SET program if eligible.
The supervised clinical assessment period is how RACS confirms competency and addresses identified gaps before recommending specialist recognition.
A RACS area of need assessment is tied to a specific area-of-need position and evaluates the applicant's suitability as well as whether the position has adequate resources.
The RACS area of need assessment normally takes up to around four months from activation.

faq.question: What is FRACS?
faq.answerMarkdown: Fellowship of the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons, awarded to surgeons who complete RACS training (or are assessed as comparable) across the nine recognised specialties. It's the basis on which an overseas-trained surgeon is recommended for specialist registration.

faq.question: Does RACS or the Medical Board grant my registration?
faq.answerMarkdown: RACS assesses comparability and recommends what you must do; the Medical Board makes the registration decision. RACS is the assessing college, the Board is the regulator.

faq.question: What does 'substantially comparable' require?
faq.answerMarkdown: Generally up to 12 months of clinical assessment (Medical Board Level 3 progressing to Level 4) with professional development and any up-skilling. The Fellowship Examination may be waived where you've completed a comparable exit exam or sufficient practice. Completion entitles you to specialist recognition and Fellowship.

faq.question: What's the difference between the specialist and area-of-need assessments?
faq.answerMarkdown: A specialist assessment evaluates overall comparability and isn't tied to one job. An area-of-need assessment is tied to a specific position government has classified as an area of need. Both feed a Medical Board registration decision.

faq.question: Can surgeons use the Expedited Specialist pathway?
faq.answerMarkdown: Not currently. As of 2026 the Board's expedited list covers specialties such as general practice, anaesthesia, psychiatry and obstetrics and gynaecology. Surgery isn't on it (general surgery and otolaryngology are only flagged as priorities being considered), so overseas-trained surgeons use the standard specialist pathway with RACS as the assessing college.

faq.question: Will I have to sit the RACS Fellowship Examination?
faq.answerMarkdown: It depends on your outcome. Partially comparable SIMGs must pass it; substantially comparable SIMGs may have it waived where they've completed a comparable exit exam or sufficient practice. Your individual requirements are set by RACS.
